note_data.title: Acids and bases
keyPoints: **Acids** are substances that produce hydrogen ions (H+) when dissolved in water
**Bases** are substances that react with acids to form salt and water only
**Alkalis** are bases that are soluble in water and produce hydroxide ions (OH-)
**pH scale** ranges from 0-14, with acids below 7, neutral at 7, and alkalis above 7
**Neutralisation** is the reaction between an acid and a base to produce salt and water only
**Universal indicator** shows pH by changing colour: red (pH 1-3), orange (pH 4-6), green (pH 7), blue (pH 8-11), purple (pH 12-14)
Strong acids like **hydrochloric acid** are completely ionised in water, weak acids like **ethanoic acid** are partially ionised
Common acids include **hydrochloric acid (HCl)**, **sulfuric acid (H2SO4)** and **nitric acid (HNO3)**
Common alkalis include **sodium hydroxide (NaOH)**, **potassium hydroxide (KOH)** and **ammonia solution (NH3)**

definitions.definition: A substance that produces hydrogen ions (H+) when dissolved in water
A substance that reacts with acids to form salt and water only
A base that is soluble in water and produces hydroxide ions (OH-)
A measure of the concentration of hydrogen ions in solution
The reaction between an acid and a base to produce salt and water only

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give the exact definition that matches the mark scheme
Give detailed observations including colour changes and pH values

modelAnswer.question: Explain why sodium hydroxide is described as an alkali but copper oxide is described as a base. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Sodium hydroxide is an alkali because it is soluble in water and produces hydroxide ions (OH-) when dissolved. Copper oxide is a base because it reacts with acids to form salt and water only, but it is not an alkali because it is insoluble in water.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Sodium hydroxide is soluble in water
Mark 3: Copper oxide is insoluble in water / does not dissolve

commonMistakes: Never say 'acids contain hydrogen' — always say 'acids produce hydrogen ions when dissolved in water'
Students often confuse bases and alkalis — remember all alkalis are bases but not all bases are alkalis
Never write H+ and OH- without mentioning they are ions
Don't say 'neutralisation produces salt and water and heat' — only mention salt and water in the definition
Students write 'pH 7 is neutral' but forget to state what acidic and alkaline pH ranges are
examinerTips: This topic appears every year in Paper 2 — often combined with salts preparation
Always use the exact phrase 'salt and water only' when defining neutralisation
pH questions often ask for specific colour changes with universal indicator
Learn the difference between strong/weak acids and concentrated/dilute acids
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: pH scale diagram showing 0-14 with colours: red (0-3), orange (4-6), green (7), blue (8-11), purple (12-14). Label examples at each range.
